Glossary About
Analytic Philosophy of Mind

Wilfrid Sellars

1912 – 1989

Manifest and scientific images, sapience, and the normative form of thought.

I am taking “Sellars” to mean Wilfrid Sellars. The report below treats his philosophy of mind as a synoptic system assembled from his major discussions of the Given, inner episodes, sensation, personhood, the manifest/scientific images, Aristotelian soul-theory, and Platonic-theological motifs.

Sellars’s philosophy of mind is best read as a synoptic, anti-foundationalist, realist, normatively inflected naturalism. It is not eliminativism about mind. It is not Cartesian dualism. It is not ordinary reductive materialism. It is also not a simple appeal to “emergence” in the usual twentieth-century sense. Sellars tries to show how several things can be true at once: there really are inner episodes; thought is conceptually and semantically structured; sensory consciousness is real and not just verbal behavior; persons are irreducibly normative beings; and the eventual scientific image has ontological authority over the manifest image. The unity of these claims is what he calls the philosophical task of understanding how things “hang together” in the broadest sense.

The central distinction is this: Sellars’s “mind” is not one thing. It has at least three registers. First, there is sapience: concept-using, judgment-making, norm-governed mindedness. Second, there is sentience: sensory consciousness, sensings, impressions, qualitative states. Third, there is personhood: membership in a normative community of rights, duties, intentions, reasons, and self-interpretation. The mistake he repeatedly combats is the temptation to collapse all three into one metaphysical package called “the mind,” “consciousness,” or “the soul.”

Sellars’s mature position can be summarized this way: thought is functionally and semantically role-governed, modeled on overt linguistic performances and in principle identifiable with neurophysiological processes; sensation is a real non-intentional dimension of the world that standard physicalist ontology has trouble accommodating; personhood is not a descriptive biological category but a normative status within a community. This makes his philosophy of mind unusually complex: he is more naturalist than many Kantian or phenomenological readers, but more anti-reductionist than many analytic physicalists.

1. The basic framework: manifest image, scientific image, and stereoscopic vision

Sellars’s philosophy of mind must be placed inside his distinction between the manifest image and the scientific image. The manifest image is not mere naïve common sense. Sellars treats it as a disciplined, refined, historically developed framework in which we encounter and describe ourselves as persons, agents, perceivers, thinkers, and members of communities. The scientific image, by contrast, explains the world through theoretical entities and structures often inaccessible to ordinary perception. The task is not simply to throw away the manifest image, but to achieve a “stereoscopic” view in which the scientific image has ontological authority while the manifest image, especially its framework of persons, retains indispensability.

The key point for philosophy of mind is that the manifest image’s primary objects are persons. Sellars argues that the original manifest image begins from a world understood in personalistic terms and then gradually “depersonalizes” much of nature. Rocks, storms, trees, and animals may lose full personal status; human beings retain it. This genealogical point matters: Sellars does not think personhood is a late occult addition to a fundamentally impersonal world-picture. The category of the person is basic to the manifest image, even though the scientific image describes human beings as complex physical systems.

This generates the major tension. The scientific image gives us human beings as organisms, nervous systems, and ultimately complex physical processes. The manifest image gives us persons who deliberate, promise, infer, obey rules, take responsibility, and occupy positions in a normative community. Sellars’s answer is not reduction but joining. Person-talk is not to be reconstructed as ordinary descriptive scientific talk. It is a normative framework that must be integrated with, rather than replaced by, the scientific image.

2. The critique of the Given: no epistemic foundation in raw consciousness

Sellars’s most famous contribution to philosophy of mind and epistemology is his critique of the Myth of the Given. The target is not merely sense-data theory. It is the broader idea that there are immediate episodes of awareness that are intrinsically epistemic: self-authenticating bits of knowledge that require no conceptual background, no inferential setting, no norms of correctness, and no learned place in a public logical space. Sellars denies this. He does not deny that we have sensations. He denies that sensations, simply by occurring, amount to knowledge.

This is decisive for consciousness. For Sellars, there may be nonconceptual sensory episodes, including episodes in infants or animals, but such episodes are not yet knowledge. Knowledge requires placement in what he famously calls the logical space of reasons. A report such as “this is red” is not merely caused by a red object or by a red sensation. It is a move in a normative practice: it can be justified, challenged, corrected, and assessed. Sellars therefore rejects the picture in which consciousness first gives us private certainties and public language is later built on top of them.

This is the root of his “psychological nominalism.” Sellars denies that awareness of kinds, facts, or logical relations is available prior to and independently of language-learning. Awareness in the relevant epistemic sense belongs to a conceptual order. That does not mean that sensations are linguistic. It means that knowing that one has a sensation, or classifying an episode as a sensation of a certain kind, requires concepts whose home is a public normative practice.

This is why Sellars is not a classical empiricist. Experience can be part of the causal story of knowledge, but it cannot serve as an autonomous epistemic foundation. A perceptual report is authoritative only within a network of concepts, background knowledge, reliability conditions, and norms of correction. Empirical knowledge is rational not because it rests on incorrigible foundations, but because it is self-correcting within a wider practice.

3. Inner episodes without Cartesianism: the Myth of Jones

Sellars’s most important constructive device is the Myth of Jones in “Empiricism and the Philosophy of Mind.” He imagines a community whose language is originally Rylean: it can speak of public objects, public behavior, and dispositions, but not yet of inner thoughts and sensations. A theoretical genius, Jones, postulates inner episodes to explain intelligent behavior that occurs without overt speech. This is “anthropological science fiction,” but its philosophical purpose is serious: it shows how talk of inner episodes can arise without being grounded in a private Given.

Jones first introduces thoughts as theoretical inner episodes modeled on overt speech. These episodes are not little ghostly utterances, nor are they necessarily verbal images. They are posited states whose functional and semantic roles are modeled on the roles of public linguistic performances. Thus, to think that snow is white is not to contemplate a private object. It is to be in an inner state that plays a role analogous to the public assertion “snow is white.”

This gives Sellars a distinctive view of intentionality. Thoughts have intentionality because they inherit, in theoretical transposition, the semantic and inferential roles of public language. Meaning is not a primitive mental relation between an inner item and an external object. Semantic statements are role-statements: they locate expressions within patterns of use, inference, and rule-governed behavior. Sellars’s view is therefore a precursor to later inferentialist and functional-role theories of meaning, but with a stronger scientific-realist background than many later pragmatist appropriations.

The Myth of Jones also accounts for privileged access without Cartesian foundations. Once a community has learned the theory of inner episodes, subjects can be trained to make direct first-person reports: “I am thinking that p,” “I have a red sensation,” “I am in pain.” These reports may be non-inferential in ordinary practice, but they are not epistemically primitive in the mythical foundationalist sense. Their authority depends on the prior public availability of the concepts in which they are framed.

This is a subtle middle position. Sellars does not deny privacy, introspection, or inner episodes. A useful secondary summary puts it well: he retains something from the Cartesian picture, including private episodes and direct self-knowledge, but rejects substance dualism, total introspective transparency, and the idea that private episodes ground the public world.

4. Thought, mind, and conceptual consciousness

Sellars’s theory of thought is anti-Cartesian in two ways. First, thoughts are not ontologically primitive mental substances or acts of a soul-substance. Second, their intentionality is not explained by a private act of meaning. Thoughts are theoretical states of language-using organisms, introduced to explain intelligent behavior, and individuated by their functional-semantic role.

In “Philosophy and the Scientific Image of Man,” Sellars says that thoughts are introduced by analogy with speech. Once this analogy is in place, the thought episodes need not retain any mysterious qualitative remainder. They can, in principle, be identified with neurophysiological processes, provided those processes play the right role in the explanatory economy of rational behavior.

This makes Sellars comparatively friendly to functionalism about thought, though not in the later computationalist sense alone. He is interested in the role of inner episodes in a normative-linguistic economy. Conceptual thinking is not merely causal information-processing. It is rule-governed, inferentially articulated, and answerable to standards of correctness. A creature counts as rational not merely because it has habits, but because it can be understood as acting under rules. Sellars’s “Language, Rules and Behavior” dramatizes this with a theological joke about God whispering rules to Adam; the point is not a doctrine of revelation but the idea that rationality is constituted by responsiveness to rules rather than by mere conditioning.

Sellars therefore gives us a philosophy of conceptual consciousness in which awareness-as is conceptually structured. To be aware of something as red, as triangular, as a promise, as a reason, or as a duty is not merely to undergo a sensory state. It is to classify, judge, or respond within a space of norms. This is why his philosophy of mind cannot be extracted from epistemology, semantics, and social philosophy. Mind, at the level of sapience, is essentially normative.

5. Sensation and sensory consciousness: the hard part in Sellars

Sellars’s treatment of sensation is much less reducible than his treatment of thought. He repeatedly says that the ordinary mind-body problem is often really a sensorium-body problem. Conceptual states and sensory states differ radically. Conceptual consciousness involves awareness-as, classification, judgment, and the logical space of reasons. Sensory consciousness involves sensings, impressions, qualitative episodes, and the manifold of sense. Sellars explicitly notes that his title “Is Consciousness Physical?” chiefly concerns sensory consciousness; conceptual consciousness requires a different dialectic.

This is one of the most important points for any reconstruction of Sellars’s philosophy of mind. He is not the kind of philosopher who thinks that once we have explained intentionality, we have explained consciousness. Thought and sensation are different problems. Thoughts can be modeled on linguistic performances and eventually identified with neurophysiological role-players. Sensations cannot be handled so easily, because they involve qualitative character.

In the Myth of Jones, Sellars introduces impressions as inner states of perceivers. He rejects the classical picture of sensations as private colored particulars, such as inner red patches or mental wafers. An impression of a red triangle is not itself a little red triangular object. Rather, it is a state of a perceiver modeled by analogy with the properties of perceptible objects. It has a structure of resemblance and difference corresponding to the structure of sensible qualities.

This lets Sellars reject both sense-data foundationalism and crude behaviorism. Sensations are real, but they are not epistemically Given. They are inner states, but their concepts can be publicly introduced and intersubjectively disciplined. First-person reports of sensations have special authority, but that authority is embedded in a public language whose concepts are not derived from private acquaintance.

The result is a layered account. At the causal level, sensory episodes are produced by organisms interacting with environments. At the conceptual level, we learn to report and classify them. At the ontological level, Sellars eventually argues that the scientific image must be expanded or reinterpreted to accommodate the qualitative character of sensing. This last point is where his late philosophy becomes most metaphysically ambitious.

6. Color, sensa, and the ontology of sensory consciousness

Sellars’s treatment of color is central. He thinks that ordinary manifest objects are not, in the final scientific sense, colored as they appear. Saying that physical objects are not really colored is not, for him, a simple empirical discovery inside common sense. It is a critique of the common-sense framework from the standpoint of a more adequate scientific ontology.

But Sellars does not simply eliminate color. He relocates it. The manifest image treats color as a property of public objects. The scientific image, as usually conceived, gives us particles, fields, neurophysiology, and causal processes, but not the homogeneous qualitative character of a pink expanse or a blue sensing. Sellars’s mature problem is that sensory qualities seem to require a place in reality, but not as properties of ordinary external objects.

In “Is Consciousness Physical?” Sellars considers several options: reductive materialism, emergent materialism, epiphenomenalism, Cartesianism, and a more radical process ontology. He rejects simple reductive materialism because sensory qualities cannot be straightforwardly identified with physical states as ordinarily conceived. He also resists epiphenomenalism, because it makes sensa causally idle.

His late answer is a theory of absolute processes. Instead of treating the basic furniture of reality as enduring objects with properties, he considers an ontology in which object-like entities are patterns of more basic processual occurrences. Within such an ontology, sensory processes—his “s-ings,” such as reddings or C-sharp-ings—can be real constituents of the world. They are not physical in the narrower “physical2” sense of being definable within the ontology of non-sentient physical science, but they are physical in the broader “physical1” sense of belonging to the spatiotemporal causal order.

This is one of the most underappreciated features of Sellars’s philosophy of mind. He is not a standard identity theorist about qualia. He is not a phenomenalist. He is not a dualist. He is not an eliminativist about sensory consciousness. He tries to construct a naturalistic ontology in which sensory qualities are real, causally efficacious, and integrated into the scientific image by transforming the metaphysics of the physical itself.

The consequence is that Sellars’s philosophy of consciousness is more radical than many “Sellarsian” philosophies of intentionality. Many later Sellarsians emphasize the space of reasons, inferentialism, and anti-Givenness. But the full Sellars also includes a speculative metaphysics of sentience in which the final scientific image may contain qualitative processes not captured by the ontology of particles or standard neurophysiology.

7. Persons: normativity, community, and the irreducibility of the “ought”

Sellars’s account of persons is not reducible to his account of thought or sensation. A person is not simply a thinking organism or a sentient organism. Personhood is a normative status. Sellars connects persons with membership in a community structured by rights, duties, intentions, and mutual recognition. He explicitly invokes Kant’s image of a community of rational beings, the Kingdom of Ends, to explain this dimension.

For Sellars, the categories pertaining to persons cannot be reconstructed as categories pertaining merely to scientific objects. This is not because persons contain supernatural stuff. It is because personhood is constituted by normative relations, and normative vocabulary is not reducible to descriptive causal vocabulary. An organism can be described scientifically; a person must also be understood as one who can undertake commitments, acknowledge obligations, and act as a member of a “we.”

This is where Sellars’s philosophy of mind becomes a philosophy of practical reason. Mind is not merely representational. To be a minded person is to occupy a place in a normative order. The “I” of thought is inseparable from the “we” of language, rule-following, and mutual accountability. Sellars’s personal subject is therefore neither a Cartesian ego nor a mere biological individual. It is a normatively instituted agent.

This has consequences for the soul. If “soul” means an immaterial substance that privately owns thoughts and sensations, Sellars gives us little support for it. If “soul” means the form of a living person’s normative and practical unity, then Sellars is surprisingly hospitable to a reconstructed, non-Cartesian soul-language.

8. Aristotelian soul: form, organism, and anti-Cartesianism

Sellars’s “Aristotelian Philosophies of Mind” is crucial because it shows that he understood “soul” not only through modern mind-body dualism but also through ancient and scholastic hylomorphism. He emphasizes that Aristotle and the scholastics did not conceive soul and body as two interacting substances. The human soul is the substantial form of the human individual; the human being acts, not the soul as a separate agent. Even if intellection were irreducible to non-intellectual processes, that would not by itself yield Cartesian dualism.

This matters enormously. Sellars’s anti-Cartesianism is not simply modern physicalism. He recognizes that the older Aristotelian tradition offers a way to speak of soul as the organization, actuality, or form of a living being rather than as a separable mental thing. In this respect, Aristotelian soul-language is closer to Sellars’s own anti-dualist instincts than Cartesian mind-language is.

A Sellarsian reconstruction of soul would therefore not begin with private consciousness. It would begin with the living, acting, norm-governed individual. The soul would not be an inner theater. It would be the formal-practical unity of an organism-person: the structured capacity to perceive, act, learn, speak, reason, intend, and participate in communal norms. This is not exactly Aristotle’s doctrine, and it is not orthodox scholastic metaphysics, but it is a Sellars-compatible appropriation of hylomorphic grammar.

The theological importance is that Sellars does not force us to identify “soul” with a ghostly substance. He makes room for a non-Cartesian theology of soul, provided that such theology can tolerate a strongly naturalistic account of organism, sensation, and language.

9. Plato, the Divine Craftsman, and the soul as craftsman

Sellars’s “The Soul as Craftsman” gives a different ancient-theological register. There he takes Plato’s Timaeus seriously as a metaphysical vision structured by craftsmanship. The Divine Craftsman, the World Soul, and the shaping activity of soul are not treated as incidental ornaments. Sellars reads craftsmanship as central to the metaphysical architecture: soul is connected with ordering, forming, animating, and making good or bad use of its own powers.

The most important idea for philosophy of mind is soul as self-shaping activity. Sellars notes that soul’s powers and propensities can be raw material for the soul itself. The “art of living” is not merely external technique; it is the formation of one’s own dispositions, capacities, and practical orientation. In this register, soul is not primarily a container of experiences but a craftsman of life.

This Platonic-theological motif connects unexpectedly well with Sellars’s Kantian account of personhood. A person is not only a locus of sensory and conceptual states. A person is a practical being whose life is assessable by norms. The theological imagery of divine and psychic craftsmanship becomes philosophically relevant as a way of thinking about norm-governed self-formation.

The point should not be overstated. Sellars is not straightforwardly endorsing Platonic theology as literal doctrine. But he is attentive to the philosophical work done by theological images: world-ordering intelligence, soul as animator, soul as practical artisan, the intelligibility of form, and the relation between rational order and living agency. These themes help reveal what modern philosophy loses when it reduces soul-talk to the Cartesian mind-body problem.

10. Descartes, God, and the relocation of sensible qualities

Sellars’s comments on Descartes and God also matter. In correspondence with J. J. C. Smart, Sellars discusses the Cartesian treatment of impressions, color, and divine creative activity. Descartes removes color from the external physical world and treats sensory impressions as ultimate in a way tied to his broader theological metaphysics. Sellars rejects that framework, but he does not simply conclude that color is unreal. Rather, he relocates sensible qualities into the ideal scientific image as features of sensa or sensory processes intertwined with central nervous system processes.

This is a good example of how theological comments should be handled. The reference to God is not a detachable historical curiosity. It clarifies the metaphysical function of the Cartesian system: God guarantees a world in which the mind’s clear and distinct ideas and sensory impressions have a certain status. Sellars’s alternative removes that theological machinery but still faces the same philosophical question: where, in the real world, do sensory qualities belong?

Thus Sellars’s naturalism is not simply secularized Cartesianism. He does not retain an inner realm of primitive mental qualities and subtract God. He reconstructs the entire account: public language first, theoretical inner episodes, non-Given introspection, sensory states rather than sense-data particulars, and finally a process ontology in which qualitative sensings can be real without being ghostly.

11. Theology as conceptual genealogy, not distraction

Taken together, Sellars’s theological and quasi-theological references do three kinds of work.

First, they provide genealogies of metaphysical grammar. “Soul,” “form,” “craft,” “world-soul,” “Kingdom of Ends,” and “divine creation” are inherited conceptual structures through which philosophy has tried to understand agency, life, normativity, and consciousness. Sellars is too historically alert to treat them as mere decorations.

Second, they provide alternative models to Cartesian dualism. Aristotelian soul as substantial form and Platonic soul as craftsman both resist the picture of mind as a private immaterial substance interacting with a body. These older models are not simply Sellars’s own doctrine, but they are philosophically closer to his anti-Cartesian project than the modern “inner theater” view.

Third, they mark the boundary between scientific description and normative-practical status. Sellars’s Kantian invocation of a community of rational beings shows that personhood involves the “ought,” not merely the “is.” This is not theology in the narrow confessional sense, but it belongs to the same deep tradition in which rational persons are more than objects in nature.

A Sellarsian philosophy of soul should therefore reject the soul as a private Cartesian substance but preserve the soul as a name for the living person’s formal, normative, self-shaping unity. Theological discourse becomes relevant where it articulates that unity: formation, vocation, rational community, the art of living, and the non-reducibility of persons to mechanisms.

12. Non-English reception and interpretive pressure points

Some non-Anglophone discussions bring out dimensions that are less prominent in standard Anglo-American Sellars reception.

An Italian study by Francesco Gusmano, for example, reads Sellars’s manifest/scientific image dialectic as a challenge to any simple naturalization of the human. It stresses that the scientific image is not one unified finished object but a plurality of scientific images—physics, biology, physiology, psychology, sociology—and argues that persons, moral life, and consciousness put pressure on reductive naturalization. This reading is valuable because it highlights the existential and anthropological stakes of Sellars’s project, though it risks underplaying Sellars’s own attempt to give sensory consciousness a place inside an expanded scientific ontology.

A Spanish-language presentation of John McDowell’s reading of “Empiricism and the Philosophy of Mind” emphasizes that Sellars’s attack is directed at traditional empiricism: experience as an inner episode can yield knowledge only against the background of other empirical knowledge and conceptual capacities. This is close to the Anglo reception, but the Spanish context usefully reinforces the point that Sellars is not denying experience; he is denying experience’s autonomous foundational role.

A French discussion of Sellars, Kant, and the “Pittsburgh School” highlights the relevance of inner sense, apperception, and process ontology for accommodating qualitative facts of consciousness. That is an important corrective to readings that treat Sellars almost exclusively as a philosopher of inferential norms. His late process ontology is not a peripheral oddity if one wants his full philosophy of consciousness.

German-language framing also matters because “Philosophie des Geistes” can mislead English readers: “Geist” in this context does not simply mean religious spirit or soul, but the domain of the mental. That lexical distinction is useful when comparing Sellars’s “mind,” “spirit,” “soul,” and “person” vocabularies across traditions.

Finally, a Polish/European theological-philosophical context represented by Józef Bremer’s work in Scientia et Fides uses Sellars’s manifest/scientific image distinction to reconsider whether Aristotelian soul-language remains useful in cognitive science. The reported argument is that Cartesian mind and some contemporary self-theories are narrower than Aristotelian soul, and that emergence-talk may have heuristic value for cognitive explanation. This is directly relevant to the user’s concern: Sellars can function as a bridge between philosophy of mind, cognitive science, and non-Cartesian theological anthropology.

The non-English material does not overturn the primary texts, but it shifts emphasis. Anglo reception often centers on the Myth of the Given, inferentialism, and the space of reasons. Continental and theological-adjacent receptions more readily notice the anthropological, hylomorphic, and consciousness-related dimensions: personhood, soul, form, lived normativity, and the limits of scientific objectification.

13. Extracted Sellarsian philosophy of mind: twelve theses

A comprehensive Sellarsian philosophy of mind can be extracted as follows.

First, the mental is not the Given. Inner episodes exist, but they do not carry intrinsic epistemic authority simply by occurring. Sensation is real; knowledge requires conceptual placement.

Second, mind divides into sapience and sentience. Conceptual consciousness and sensory consciousness are different problems. A theory of intentionality is not yet a theory of qualitative consciousness.

Third, thought is modeled on language. Inner conceptual episodes are theoretical posits modeled on overt linguistic performances. Their intentionality derives from their role in a system of norms, inferences, and semantic practices.

Fourth, intentionality is not a primitive private relation. Meaning is not a ghostly mental pointing. It is constituted by functional, inferential, and rule-governed roles within public practices.

Fifth, introspection is real but derivative. First-person authority is compatible with the public origin of mental concepts. Self-knowledge does not require the Myth of the Given.

Sixth, sensations are states, not private objects. Sellars rejects classical sense-data as inner particulars. An impression of red is not a private red thing; it is a sensory state whose structure can be modeled by analogy with public sensible qualities.

Seventh, qualitative consciousness is not eliminated. Sellars relocates sensible qualities rather than denying them. The final scientific image may need to include qualitative sensory processes.

Eighth, ordinary physicalism is too narrow. If “physical” means only the ontology of current non-sentient physical science, then sensory consciousness may not be physical in that sense. If “physical” means belonging to the spatiotemporal causal order, then sensory consciousness can be physical.

Ninth, persons are normative beings. Personhood is not merely biological organization plus consciousness. It is membership in a community of commitments, rights, duties, and shared intentions.

Tenth, the scientific image has ontological authority, but the manifest image supplies the person-framework. We must ultimately join the language of persons to the scientific image rather than reduce personhood to object-description.

Eleventh, soul-language is acceptable only when de-Cartesianized. Sellars is hostile to soul as a separate mental substance, but sympathetic to older grammars in which soul means form, animation, practical unity, or self-shaping rational life.

Twelfth, theology is relevant when it articulates form, normativity, craftsmanship, rational community, and the art of living. It becomes misleading when it reintroduces a private immaterial ego as the owner of Given mental contents.

14. Main tensions and open problems

The first major tension is language and prelinguistic mentality. Sellars can allow sensations in infants and animals, but conceptual awareness and knowledge require language-like normative capacities. This creates pressure around animal cognition, infant cognition, and nonlinguistic intentionality. A strict Sellarsian will distinguish sensory discrimination from conceptual awareness; critics may find this too restrictive.

The second tension is qualia and process ontology. Sellars’s late account of s-ings is powerful because it refuses both eliminativism and dualism. But it is metaphysically demanding. It asks us to revise the ontology of the physical, treating object-like entities as patterns of absolute processes and making sensory qualities constituents of the causal order. That is not a small adjustment to physicalism.

The third tension is normativity and naturalism. Sellars says the person-framework must be joined to the scientific image, not reduced to it. But how exactly this joining works remains difficult. The scientific image describes causes; the manifest image includes obligations, reasons, and communal intentions. Sellars’s answer is not a completed theory so much as a disciplined refusal of two bad options: supernatural dualism and reductive scientism.

The fourth tension is theological appropriation. Aristotelian and Platonic soul-language can be fruitfully reconstructed through Sellars, but only at a price. A Sellars-compatible theology of soul would need to be anti-Cartesian, anti-Given, socially and normatively grounded, and open to scientific realism about embodiment. It could preserve soul as form, vocation, practical selfhood, or rational-personal unity. It would have difficulty preserving soul as a private, self-transparent, immaterial substance whose mental contents are epistemically foundational.

15. Final synthesis

Sellars’s philosophy of mind is a three-level theory of sentient organisms, sapient thinkers, and normative persons.

At the lowest relevant level, human beings are organisms embedded in causal nature. They undergo sensory states. These states are not epistemic foundations, but they are real. In his mature metaphysics, Sellars tries to make room for them as qualitative processes within an expanded scientific image.

At the conceptual level, human beings are language-using rational agents. Their thoughts are not private Cartesian objects but inner functional-semantic episodes whose roles are modeled on public speech, inference, and rule-governed practice.

At the personal level, human beings are members of a normative community. They are not merely things that think and feel. They undertake commitments, acknowledge reasons, obey and revise rules, form intentions, and participate in a shared “we.” This is the level at which Sellars’s philosophy of mind becomes a philosophy of personhood.

The soul, reconstructed Sellarsianly, is not the ghost in the machine. It is the living person’s normative-formal unity: embodied, sentient, concept-using, rule-governed, historically formed, and capable of self-crafting. Theology becomes relevant not by supplying an occult substance, but by preserving older insights into form, rational community, moral vocation, and the art of living. Sellars gives us a naturalist philosophy of mind, but not a flattening one. He eliminates the Given, not consciousness; rejects Cartesian soul-substance, not personhood; criticizes ordinary manifest ontology, not the indispensability of the human normative world.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
